title: SRP Bluffcatch River
prompt: SRP, you called preflop in the BB with Jh9h. Board runs out Js 7c 3d 2h Jd. Villain bets 75% pot on the river. The top card paired on the river. Do you call or fold?

answer.explanation: You have trips with a decent kicker. The paired top card on the river reduces villain's value combos. Even vs Pool B which underbluffs, trips is too strong to fold.

diagnostic_prompts.prompt: What bluffs still reach this river after the turn checks through?
diagnostic_prompts.expected_reasoning: The turn check keeps in delayed floats and missed backdoor pressure, so trips still need to defend.

options.label: Mostly boats and trapped value get here, so trips should overfold.
Delayed floats and missed backdoors still arrive here, so high trips stay in the bluff-catcher region.
The size is large enough that the range details barely matter.

coaching_context.key_concept: Trips on a paired river should defend more often than the raw bet size suggests.
coaching_context.range_context: After the turn checks through, BTN still arrives with thin value, delayed bluffs, and missed backdoor floats instead of only full houses.
range_notes: Jh9h sits high in BB's bluff-catching region because it blocks some value boats while still beating all missed backdoor pressure hands.
BTN's strongest value is partially capped by the turn check, so the river size alone cannot force trips into an overfold.

what_changed_by_street.detail: The small flop c-bet keeps BTN's range wide instead of immediately polar.
The turn check removes some pure triple-barrel value and preserves more air that must decide whether to fire river.
The paired jack removes value combos while preserving enough missed floats to make trips a necessary bluff-catcher.

coaching_context.difficulty_reason: Trips feels automatic, but paired rivers are really about value-combo removal and how the turn action filtered range density.
coaching_context.why_preferred_line_works: Jh9h is simply too high in range after the turn checks through, and it blocks enough strong value that folding would over-defend villain's bluffs.
coaching_context.common_mistake: Folding because the large river size looks scary without rechecking how many natural value hands survived the turn check.
common_mistakes: Treating any paired river overbet as value-heavy even when the prior street checked through.
Ignoring that trips blocks some of villain's natural value continuations while still beating missed delayed floats.
coaching_context.follow_up: Review paired-river bluff-catching spots where the turn checked through before a late large bet.

value_buckets.label: Boats and trapped nutted value
value_buckets.note: These are the cleanest hands that still want a large river bet after the turn checks through.

value_buckets.label: Trips for thinner value
value_buckets.note: Trips exist, but not every jack wants the same polar size once the turn checked back.

bluff_buckets.label: Missed delayed floats
bluff_buckets.note: The turn check leaves in backdoor floats and gutters that can still fire once checked to on the river.

bluff_catchers.label: High trips that must defend
bluff_catchers.note: These combos sit high enough to continue against the surviving missed-float bluffs.

bluff_catchers.label: One-pair bluff catchers below threshold
bluff_catchers.note: These hands lose too much to the remaining value region once the size polarizes.

combo_groups.label: Hands that dislike the turn check-through
combo_groups.note: The skipped turn removes some of the cleanest value pressure Villain would normally bring to river.

threshold_notes: Jh9h is above the river defense threshold because it beats every missed float and blocks part of the strongest jack-x and boat region.
blocker_notes: Holding a jack trims Villain's trip value while leaving the missed backdoor floats intact, which is exactly what you want from a bluff-catcher.

hero_hand_bucket.label: This combo
hero_hand_bucket.note: Strong enough to call, but only because the line still contains enough air after the turn checks through.

title: SRP Bluffcatch River
prompt: SRP, you called preflop IP with Ah5h. Board: Ks Qd 7c 4s As. River brings the ace as a scare card. Villain bets 66% pot. You have top pair weak kicker. Call or fold?

answer.explanation: Ace on river is a scare card but it gave you top pair. Villain's bluffs increased since the ace blocks AK/AQ. Call is correct even vs Pool B.

title: SRP Bluffcatch River
prompt: SRP, you defend BB with Tc9c. Board: Th 6s 3c 2d 5h. Four-liner straight possible (2-3-5-6 connects with 4). Villain bets 80% pot on the river. You have top pair. Call or fold vs Pool B?

answer.explanation: Four-liner on the river means villain can credibly have straights. Pool B underbluffs big rivers. Top pair is a fold when the board runs out this connected.

title: Facing Turn Overbet
prompt: SRP, you're OOP with As Qd on Kh 9c 4d. Villain checks back flop. Turn: 2s. You check, villain overbets 150% pot. You have two overcards and a backdoor. What do you do?

answer.explanation: Facing a turn overbet with just overcards is a clear fold. Pool B under-bluffs overbets, so this is heavily weighted toward value. You have no pair and poor equity.

title: Facing Turn Overbet
prompt: SRP, you're OOP with 8s 8d on 7h 5c 3d. You check, villain c-bets 33%, you call. Turn: 9s. You check, villain overbets 125% pot. You have an overpair. Call or fold?

answer.explanation: You have an overpair on a low board. Even facing a turn overbet, 88 is near the top of your range. Pool B leans toward value overbets, but you're too strong to fold here.

title: Facing Turn Overbet
prompt: SRP, you're OOP with Jh Td on Qc 8s 4h. You check-call flop c-bet. Turn: 6d. Villain overbets 140% pot. You have an OESD. What's your play?

answer.explanation: With an OESD you have 8 outs (~16% equity). The pot is offering enough to call. Check-raise all-in is also viable as a semi-bluff if stacks are right.

title: River Raise as Bluff
prompt: SRP, you're IP with 6s 5s on Ks 9d 3c 7h. Villain bets turn 50% pot, you call with OESD. River: 2d. Flush missed, straight missed. Villain bets 50% pot. Raise as bluff?

answer.explanation: You have zero showdown value with missed draws. Raising the river as a bluff is profitable here because you can credibly rep strong hands. Pool B under-calls river raises.

title: River Raise as Bluff
prompt: SRP, you're IP with Qh Jh on Ah 8c 3h 5d Th. Flush and straight complete on river. Villain checks. You have a flush. Should you overbet for value?

answer.explanation: With a very strong queen-high flush on a board where straights also complete, you still hold a premium polar value hand. Overbet is correct to extract max value from worse flushes, straights, two pair, and sticky bluff-catchers.

coaching_context.key_concept: Overbets are best when the river shifts nut advantage sharply toward the aggressor.
coaching_context.range_context: BTN arrives with all strong heart combos and some straights, while BB is capped by the earlier check-call line and still has many bluff-catchers.
range_notes: QhJh is not the nut flush, but it is still high enough in BTN's river value region to support a polar overbet.
The river completes both flushes and straights, which lets BTN credibly overbet a narrow, powerful range without pretending every flush is the nuts.

what_changed_by_street.detail: BTN starts with a small c-bet that keeps BB wide.
The second barrel keeps pressure on one-pair hands and draws while preserving a strong river-polar range.
The heart river collapses BTN's value into a premium polar region that can size up aggressively.

coaching_context.difficulty_reason: Many players underbet premium river value because the made hand feels strong enough already, missing how much the river shifts BTN into a polar value region.
coaching_context.why_preferred_line_works: A very strong flush on a coordinated river still wants a polar size because worse flushes, straights, and bluff-catchers continue too often versus smaller bets.
coaching_context.common_mistake: Choosing a medium value size because the board looks scary, even though the scare card actually favors the bettor.
common_mistakes: Failing to distinguish between a strong hand and a true nut-advantaged overbet candidate.
Checking back rivers that dramatically improve the aggressor's polar region.
coaching_context.follow_up: Review more river overbet-for-value spots where the final card shifts nut advantage strongly toward the aggressor.

value_buckets.label: Premium flushes
value_buckets.note: These are the cleanest overbet candidates because they dominate the calling range once the river heart lands.

value_buckets.label: Strong straights and sets
value_buckets.note: These hands still value bet often, but they do not own the same nut advantage as the strongest flushes.

combo_groups.label: Hands that can pay an overbet
combo_groups.note: BB still arrives with enough second-best made hands that a polar value size prints.

threshold_notes: QhJh is well above the value-threshold for a polar river size even without being the absolute nut flush.
blocker_notes: QhJh removes some of BB's strongest flush continues while still leaving plenty of second-best flushes and straights to pay.

hero_hand_bucket.label: This combo
hero_hand_bucket.note: It is premium enough to polarize, even if only the king-high flush technically sits above it.

title: River Raise as Bluff
prompt: SRP, you're IP with 9c 8c on Tc 7d 2s 4c Jc. Board has a four-liner (7-8-9-T-J possible straight). Villain bets 60% pot. You made a flush. Do you raise?

answer.explanation: You have a flush on a four-liner board. Raising is for value since villain may also have a straight. The four-liner adds credibility to raising range.

title: Turn Probe Bet
prompt: SRP, villain raised BTN and you called BB. Villain checks back flop on Kd 8h 3c. Turn: 7d (second diamond). You're OOP with Ad Td. Should you probe bet?

answer.explanation: The turn diamond gives you a flush draw plus overcard. Probing is correct because villain showed weakness by checking back, and the flush draw turn favors your range.

diagnostic_prompts.prompt: Why does AdTd want to probe the turn instead of checking again?
diagnostic_prompts.expected_reasoning: The diamond turn shifts leverage, and this combo clears the probing threshold because it combines fold equity with strong backup equity.

options.label: Ace-high already has enough showdown value, so betting mostly burns equity.
The turn shifts leverage to BB, and this combo has enough draw strength to pressure capped checks.
The ace of diamonds matters by itself, even if the hand had no real draw backup.

coaching_context.key_concept: Turn probes punish capped flop checks when the new card helps the caller more than the preflop raiser.
coaching_context.range_context: BB keeps all suited backdoor diamonds and stronger king-x after the flop checks through, while BTN gives up some immediate c-bet leverage.
range_notes: AdTd combines overcard equity with a strong nut-draw candidate, so it can bet for fold equity and equity realization at the same time.
The second diamond is better for the caller than for the delayed-checking raiser, which gives BB more incentive to seize initiative.

what_changed_by_street.detail: BTN checking back caps some top-pair density and leaves more immediate folds in range.
The second diamond shifts nut potential toward BB and creates a clean probe window.

coaching_context.difficulty_reason: Ace-high feels too weak to bet for many students, even when the turn creates one of the cleanest equity-denial probe cards in the node.
coaching_context.why_preferred_line_works: AdTd can win immediately versus capped flop checks and still has robust equity when called.
coaching_context.common_mistake: Checking because ace-high looks like showdown value instead of seeing the turn as a leverage card for the caller.
common_mistakes: Missing that turn probes are often driven by range shift, not by already-made hand strength.
Failing to separate good bluff-candidates from weak give-ups after flop checks through.
coaching_context.follow_up: Review more turn probe spots where the caller gains new nut potential after flop checks through.

value_buckets.label: Strong made probes
value_buckets.note: These hands can bet confidently once BTN checks back the flop.

bluff_buckets.label: Nut-draw probes
bluff_buckets.note: These hands gain fold equity immediately and retain strong backup equity when called.

bluff_buckets.label: Weaker ace-high give-ups
bluff_buckets.note: Ace-high alone is not enough. The draw and leverage card are what make the probe work.

combo_groups.label: BTN flop checks that stay capped
combo_groups.note: The flop check-back creates the fold equity BB wants to attack on the diamond turn.

threshold_notes: AdTd is comfortably above the bluff-threshold because it has both fold equity now and robust equity realization when called.
blocker_notes: The ace of diamonds blocks some of BTN's strongest continues while also giving BB the cleanest nut-draw candidate to probe.

hero_hand_bucket.label: This combo
hero_hand_bucket.note: It is not betting because ace-high is good. It is betting because the turn shifted leverage and the draw quality is excellent.

steps.prompt: Turn decision after flop checks through.

answer.explanation: The turn diamond gives you a flush draw plus overcard. Probing is correct because villain showed weakness by checking back, and the flush draw turn favors your range.

what_changed_by_street.detail: The second diamond creates a clean leverage card for BB to probe.

coaching_context.why_preferred_line_works: AdTd now has both fold equity and meaningful backup equity when called.
common_mistakes: Passing on the probe because ace-high still feels too weak in absolute terms.

bluff_buckets.label: Turn probe candidates
bluff_buckets.note: The best probes combine overcard removal with nut-draw equity.

threshold_notes: Hands without the diamond draw fall below the natural probing threshold much faster.

hero_hand_bucket.label: Turn combo
hero_hand_bucket.note: AdTd is exactly the kind of ace-high that should convert into pressure here.

steps.prompt: River plan after the turn probe gets called and the river bricks 2c.

answer.explanation: Once the turn probe gets called and the river bricks, ace-high loses its best fold-equity story and should usually realize showdown instead of triple-barreling.

what_changed_by_street.detail: The blank river removes the semi-bluff leverage that made the turn probe attractive.

coaching_context.why_preferred_line_works: The caller's continuing range tightened on the turn, so a river bluff with ace-high now attacks too much made strength.
common_mistakes: Auto-firing the river after a good turn probe without checking whether the brick improved fold equity or not.

bluff_catchers.label: Hands that reached river too weakly
bluff_catchers.note: These hands no longer enjoy the leverage that made the turn probe attractive.

threshold_notes: Once the river bricks, ace-high without a made hand drops below the profitable bluffing threshold.

hero_hand_bucket.label: River combo
hero_hand_bucket.note: The good turn bluff does not automatically become a good river bluff.

title: Turn Probe Bet
prompt: SRP, villain checks back flop on Qs 7h 4c. Turn: 2s. You're OOP with Jc Th. Blank turn. Should you probe bet?

answer.explanation: Blank turn on a dry board. Your JT has some equity but probing into a dry texture when villain checked back is less effective. Check and re-evaluate river.

title: Turn Probe Bet
prompt: SRP, villain checks back flop on 9h 6c 5d. Turn: 8h (flush draw and straight gets there). You're OOP with Th 7h. You have the straight. How do you proceed?

answer.explanation: You flopped an OESD and turned the nuts. Bet for value on a dynamic board. 66% pot or larger is correct on this polar texture. Don't slow-play on a wet board.

title: Check-Raise Flop SRP
prompt: SRP, you're BB vs BTN open. Flop: 8c 7d 6s. Villain c-bets 33% pot. You have 9h 8h (top pair + OESD). What do you do?

answer.explanation: On a wet connected board, check-raising with top pair + OESD is textbook. You have equity when called and can credibly rep strong hands. Pool B under-defends vs check-raises.

title: Check-Raise Flop SRP
prompt: SRP, you're BB vs BTN. Flop: As Kc 3d. Villain c-bets 33% pot. You have 5h 4h (gutshot + backdoor flush). Should you check-raise?

answer.explanation: AK3 rainbow strongly favors the BTN's range. Check-raising is unprofitable on boards where villain has massive range advantage. Fold weak draws on dry, opponent-favored boards.

title: Check-Raise Flop SRP
prompt: SRP, you're BB vs BTN. Flop: Jh Td 5h. Villain c-bets 50% pot. You have Jd Ts (two pair). What's your play?

answer.explanation: Two pair on a wet board is a premium check-raise candidate. Many turn cards can kill your action or give villain free equity. Raise for value now and charge draws.

title: Triple Barrel Bluff
prompt: SRP, you raised BTN with Ac 4c, c-bet flop (Qh 8c 3c), bet turn (6d). River: Ks. You still have ace-high (missed flush). Pot is $200, you have $300 behind. Triple barrel?

answer.explanation: King on the river is a great scare card to triple barrel. You rep AK, KQ, KK. Missed flush draw has zero showdown value. Pool B over-folds to third barrels. Size large for maximum fold equity.

title: Triple Barrel Bluff
prompt: SRP, you raised CO with Kh Qh, c-bet flop (9d 7s 4c), bet turn (3h). River: 9d board pairs. Villain has called twice. Should you fire the third barrel?

answer.explanation: The paired board on the river is a great bluff card. It reduces villain's sets and trips combos while you can rep a full house. Pool B over-folds to triple barrels on scary rivers.

title: Triple Barrel Bluff
prompt: SRP, you raised BTN with Td 9d, c-bet flop (8s 5d 2d), bet turn (Jh). River: 7c completing a four-liner (5-6-7-8 or 7-8-9-T). Should you triple barrel?

answer.explanation: The four-liner river gives you a straight (6-7-8-9-T). This is actually a value bet now! But even without the straight, the four-liner would be an excellent bluff card. Bet large.

title: Facing River Donk Bet
prompt: SRP, you raised BTN and villain called in BB. Board: Kd 9h 5c 3s Kh. River pairs the king. Villain donk bets 40% pot. You have AcJc. How do you respond?

answer.explanation: Small donk bet on a paired king river is often a blocking bet with Kx. Pool B rarely donk-bluffs. With AJ high you have no showdown value and are behind the donk betting range.

title: Facing River Donk Bet
prompt: SRP, you raised BTN with Ks Qs. BB calls. Board: Th 7c 4d 2s Ac. River ace is a scare card. Villain donk bets 75% pot on the river. You have KQ high. What do you do?

answer.explanation: Large donk bet on a scare card ace river is heavily value-weighted from Pool B. They have Ax hands that checked to trap earlier streets. KQ has no showdown value. Fold.

title: Facing River Donk Bet
prompt: SRP, you raised BTN, BB calls. Board: Qd 8c 5h 3d Qc. River pairs the queen. Villain donk bets 30% pot. You have Ah Kh. Ace-high. Call, fold, or raise?

answer.explanation: Small donk on a paired queen river is often a block bet. AK has decent showdown value as it beats all missed draws. Paired board reduces Qx combos. Call the small sizing.

B.explanation: Pool B does not arrive with enough thin block bets or busted draws in this donk line, so ace-high should fold exploitatively.

coaching_context.key_concept: Small river donks on paired boards are about line composition, not just showdown rank.
coaching_context.range_context: BTN reaches river with ace-high after the turn checks through, while BB can hold thin Qx, under-realized pairs, and a small set of missed draws.
range_notes: Ace-high only works if the donk line still contains enough cheap blocker bets and missed diamonds.
When the board pairs the top card, the bettor has fewer value combos than the river texture suggests at first glance.

what_changed_by_street.detail: The small flop c-bet gets called by a wide one-pair and draw-heavy defending range.
The turn check-back leaves BTN capped and gives BB room to arrive at river with block bets.
The paired queen cuts down made-value density and makes the small donk more likely to be thin or blocking in baseline play.

coaching_context.difficulty_reason: Ace-high feels weak, but the line is shallow enough that the river decision is really about whether small donks are blocking or value-heavy.
coaching_context.why_preferred_line_works: Baseline play can still include enough thin queen-x, pocket-pair block bets, and missed diamond stabs that ace-high earns a bluff-catch at this size.
coaching_context.population_note: Pool B under-bluffs this exact donk line and leans more toward thin value than cheap blockers.
coaching_context.common_mistake: Over-folding baseline because ace-high looks trivial, or over-calling pool-specific play without checking whether the donk line is actually bluff-rich.
common_mistakes: Treating every small donk as capped weakness without checking what the prior turn check-back removed from both ranges.
Ignoring how pool tendencies can turn a baseline bluff-catch into an exploit fold even when the board looks similar.
coaching_context.follow_up: Compare more paired-river donk spots where baseline bluff-catching is acceptable but exploit pools over-value the line.

value_buckets.label: Thin queen-x and slow-played value
value_buckets.note: The paired queen trims total value density, but some thin value and traps still exist.

bluff_buckets.label: Missed diamonds and cheap blockers
bluff_buckets.note: These are the hands baseline can still turn into a small donk after the turn checks through.

bluff_catchers.label: Ace-high bluff catchers
bluff_catchers.note: The size is small enough that the top ace-highs can still pick off the thin bluff region in baseline play.

combo_groups.label: Pool B shift
combo_groups.note: In tighter pools the same donk line leans more value-heavy and less bluff-rich.

threshold_notes: AhKh is near the bluff-catching threshold: baseline can call, but a tighter pool can push the same combo just below that line.
blocker_notes: Ace-high blocks some of the missed diamond bluffs you want Villain to have, which is why this is only a thin bluff-catch even before pool adjustments.

hero_hand_bucket.label: This combo
hero_hand_bucket.note: Good enough at the small size in baseline, but not robust enough to survive every exploit pool shift.

title: Turn Delayed C-Bet
prompt: SRP, you raised BTN, BB calls. Flop: Jd 8h 3c. You check back. Turn: 4h (second heart). Should you delayed c-bet?

answer.explanation: The turn heart brings a flush draw which adds equity to your range. Delayed c-bet is good here because villain's check-call range on the flop is now under pressure from a new draw. Pool B under-defends.

title: Turn Delayed C-Bet
prompt: SRP, you raised BTN with Ah Kd, BB calls. Flop: 9c 7d 4s. You check back. Turn: As. You now have top pair top kicker. How do you size your delayed c-bet?

answer.explanation: You checked back a whiffed flop and turned top pair. This is the ideal delayed c-bet scenario. Size 66% pot for value. The ace turn polarizes your range which supports bigger sizing.

diagnostic_prompts.prompt: What changed on the turn that makes a bigger delayed c-bet better than checking again?
diagnostic_prompts.expected_reasoning: The ace turn flips range advantage back to BTN, so AK moves into a clear value region that can size up.

options.label: The flop checked through, so this line should stay passive unless the river changes things.
The ace flips the range story back to BTN, so AK can value bet firmly right away.
Pools call too wide, so checking to trap is usually stronger than rebuilding the pot.

coaching_context.key_concept: Delayed c-bets work best when the turn materially improves the aggressor after a flop range check.
coaching_context.range_context: BTN checks back many whiffs on the flop, then turns top pair top kicker on the ace, reclaiming a clear value and protection edge.
range_notes: AK is now near the top of the delayed-turn betting range and wants value from ace-x, nine-x, and sticky pocket pairs.
The ace turn is better for the preflop raiser than for the flop-checking caller, which supports a larger delayed size.

what_changed_by_street.detail: The flop check keeps BTN's range wide and disguises future top-pair turns.
The ace sharply improves BTN while also creating a card that BB must defend carefully versus.

coaching_context.difficulty_reason: Students often treat delayed c-bets as weak by default and miss when a turn card dramatically flips the leverage back to the raiser.
coaching_context.why_preferred_line_works: AK now has a strong value target and the ace turn supports a firmer size because BTN owns more top-pair advantage than BB.
coaching_context.common_mistake: Checking again because the flop checked through, even though the ace turn is exactly the kind of card that should restart aggression.
common_mistakes: Using a timid delayed size after a card that actually supports stronger polarization.
Ignoring how the flop check protects strong hands that turn top pair later.
coaching_context.follow_up: Review more delayed c-bet spots where a high overcard turn restores range advantage to the preflop raiser.

value_buckets.label: Top-pair advantage hands
value_buckets.note: These hands improve hard enough on the ace turn to justify a firmer delayed size.

bluff_buckets.label: Delayed semi-bluffs
bluff_buckets.note: The flop check keeps enough natural bluffs in range that BTN can still support a polar delayed bet.

combo_groups.label: Hands that pay the turn bet
combo_groups.note: BB still arrives with many bluff-catchers that can continue once the ace lands.

threshold_notes: AK sits comfortably above the delayed-turn value threshold, which is why the larger size is cleaner than a timid stab.
blocker_notes: Holding AK blocks some of BB's strongest ace-x continues while still targeting plenty of worse one-pair hands.

hero_hand_bucket.label: This combo
hero_hand_bucket.note: It is strong enough to restart aggression immediately once the ace flips the range picture.

steps.prompt: Turn delayed c-bet after the flop checks through.

answer.explanation: You checked back a whiffed flop and turned top pair. This is the ideal delayed c-bet scenario. Size 66% pot for value. The ace turn polarizes your range which supports bigger sizing.

what_changed_by_street.detail: The ace restores clear leverage to BTN after the flop checked through.

coaching_context.why_preferred_line_works: AK can now bet larger for value and protection instead of drifting into a second passive street.
common_mistakes: Treating the flop check as a reason to stay passive even when the turn radically changes the range picture.

value_buckets.label: Turn restart bets
value_buckets.note: The ace turn moves these hands into the clear betting bucket after the flop check-back.

hero_hand_bucket.label: Turn combo
hero_hand_bucket.note: AK should not hide behind the flop check once the ace lands.

steps.prompt: River follow-up after the delayed c-bet gets called and the river bricks 2c.

answer.explanation: After the turn delayed c-bet gets called and the river bricks, top pair top kicker still wants a thinner value bet against ace-x and sticky middle pairs.

what_changed_by_street.detail: The brick river keeps worse ace-x and bluff-catchers in range without adding many new strong hands.

coaching_context.why_preferred_line_works: A smaller river size keeps value flowing from worse one-pair hands while avoiding unnecessary polarization.
common_mistakes: Checking back too often after a delayed c-bet call because the line already feels like enough value.

combo_groups.label: River bluff-catchers that pay small
combo_groups.note: The brick river keeps enough second-best one-pair hands in range for thin value.

threshold_notes: After the delayed c-bet gets called, AK stays above the thin-value threshold but no longer wants to polarize as hard as the turn.

hero_hand_bucket.label: River combo
hero_hand_bucket.note: Still ahead often enough to bet, but no longer in the part of range that wants a huge size.

title: Turn Delayed C-Bet
prompt: SRP, BTN raise, BB calls. Flop: Kh 9c 2d. You check back with Qc Jc. Turn: Tc. You have an OESD + backdoor flush. Delayed c-bet or check?

answer.explanation: Turn gave you an OESD with backdoor flush. Delayed c-bet here as a semi-bluff is excellent. You have 8 straight outs + backdoor flush. The turn card also brings flush draws which favors your range.

title: River Value Bet Sizing
prompt: SRP, you're IP with Ah Kh on Ad 9c 5s 3h 7d. You have top pair top kicker. Villain checks river to you. Pot is $120. How should you size your value bet?

answer.explanation: TPTK is a medium-strength value hand. Smaller sizing gets called by more Ax hands and pocket pairs. Pool B calls too wide vs small river bets, so thin value small is exploitative.

title: River Value Bet Sizing
prompt: SRP, you're IP with 9s 9d on 9h 6c 3d 2s Jh. You have a set of nines. Villain checks river. Board has a paired-adjacent feel. Pot is $150. How should you size?

answer.explanation: With a set you have a clear nut hand. Overbet maximizes value from Jx, 6x, and overpairs. This is the textbook overbet opportunity — you have a huge nut advantage.

title: River Value Bet Sizing
prompt: SRP, you're IP with Kd Qd on Ks 8h 4c 2d Kc. Board has paired top card (kings). You have trip kings with Q kicker. Villain checks to you. Pot is $100. Size?

answer.explanation: Paired top card reduces villain's calling range significantly. Many Kx hands are now impossible. Size moderately (50-75%) to get value from 8x, pocket pairs, and occasional Kx that didn't 3-bet pre.

title: Facing Flop Check-Raise
prompt: SRP, you raised BTN with Qd Qc. BB calls. Flop: Jh 8c 5d. You c-bet 33%, villain check-raises to 3x. You have an overpair. What do you do?

answer.explanation: QQ is near the top of your range. On a wet J-8-5 flop, villain can check-raise with sets, two pair, and draws. Call and evaluate turn. Pool B check-raises are value-heavy but QQ is too strong to fold.

title: Facing Flop Check-Raise
prompt: SRP, you raised BTN with Ac Kc. BB calls. Flop: Td 7h 4c. You c-bet 33%, villain check-raises to 3x. You have two overcards + backdoor flush. What do you do?

answer.explanation: AKo with only backdoor equity is not strong enough to continue vs a check-raise on this texture. Pool B check-raises are heavily value-weighted. Fold and wait for a better spot.

title: Facing Flop Check-Raise
prompt: SRP, you raised BTN with 7s 6s. BB calls. Flop: 8s 5h 3c. You c-bet 33%, villain check-raises to 3x. You have an OESD. What do you do?

answer.explanation: OESD gives you 8 clean outs (~32% equity by river). You're getting the right price to call the check-raise. 3-bet shoving is also viable as a semi-bluff given fold equity. Pool B may over-fold to aggression.
